item.title: Great coup de theatre
item.content:encoded:
Until the last addendum my opinion was "unsettling, but nothing more", but the idea of a bioengeneering weapon, and the link to 1000 made all the difference in the world!
I definitey have a preference for connected stories than (even if wonderful) single items.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The first and third images that were used in this article could not be found under any source as cc-compliant. Thus, it is assumed that the images are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and have been removed. The second image comes from here and is cc-compliant.

item.title: +1+2i
item.content:encoded:
My favorite movie scene of all time is the last thirty seconds of Psycho. This article let me relive that. There's no reason an ending should wrap things up that have already been resolved. And a good "stinger" ending, like this, is incredible. It both advances the immediately-ceasing plot and recolors the big picture. So +1 for a good article and +i for a good ending.

Beyond that, one of my favorite themes, and one that I see increasingly rarely, is the idea that we are not of any importance in the grand scheme of things. That humans are a cosmic Djibouti. Sure, a lot of people know that we exist, but we're not exactly at the center of things, and our name probably means something funny in another language. So +i for striking that final chord of anti-homocentrism.

item.title: Re: Staff Post -- Open
item.content:encoded: I've updated the suggested crosslink. Please tell me what you think of the new crosslink, and how I could make it better.

item.title: Re: Staff Post -- Open
item.content:encoded: Good point. Will update the staff post with something more suitable to this article.

item.title: Re: Staff Post -- Open
item.content:encoded:
I think this:

After 1121-T-5, it's been theorized that SCP-1121 could be part of a terraforming experiment. If that is the case, one is left to wonder just what it was supposed to turn us into. If it's even us they were after.

Is the obvious place to crosslink. Why is this theorized? Are there other alien xenoforming objects or entities that seem similar?

item.title: Re: Staff Post -- Open
item.content:encoded:
In real life, bacteria can essentially breed with eachother. Mutated strains of viruses can suddenly become capable of infecting new species. And viral DNA from multiple viruses can mix together, recombine in new ways in a host, even incorporating elements of the host DNA from time to time. It's not outside of the world of possibility to have a mutated version of a virus be able to infect protozoa and bacterial cells, and then one of those infected cells also get an injection from another virus.

I do think this is sort of taking Occam's Razor and then beating a horse to death with it though. Complexity for the sake of seeing what happens is a poor writing gimmick, in my opinion.

Edit: Bacteria breeding with eachother is an entirely separate mechanism from viral transfer of DNA, I should have clarified that better.

item.title: Re: Staff Post -- Open
item.content:encoded: Alright. I see that point, sorry for the miscommunication, didn't understand it at first. Will reconsider crosslink and think of something else. Also, sorry for the late response. Something came up.

item.title: Re: Staff Post -- Open
item.content:encoded:
Yeaaahhh…that's not really what I mean. What you're proposing is the "Mr. 'Splodey" variant of science, where things are thrown together because they're similar and wouldn't it be interesting to see what happens?

My point is that this concerns a protozoa (not a virus!) that specifically targets skin. There's no indication that it would have any effect on bacteria, so why test it on them? What exactly would be the underlying hypothesis we'd be testing?

item.title: Re: Staff Post -- Open
item.content:encoded: Okay, I'll add in a possible context. Specifically, the contest I'm thinking of it being how the virus would react to other bacteria based anomalies.

item.title: Re: Staff Post -- Open
item.content:encoded:
Still sounds a little awkward. Maybe this:

and SCP-1086-1 instances are currently being considered for testing.

item.title: Re: Staff Post -- Open
item.content:encoded: I'm not a big fan of crosstests just because things are similar in nature. If we're going to add this, I'd prefer the context for crosstesting, the reason we think it might be a good idea, to be made clear.

item.title: Re: Staff Post -- Open
item.content:encoded: Got it, changed.

item.title: Re: Staff Post -- Open
item.content:encoded: "Up for consideration" sounds a bit awkward, maybe change the wording a bit to "Considered for testing"?

item.title: Staff Post -- Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Removed

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show whatever
- Hide whatever

At the end of the note at the end, change it from this:

After 1121-T-5, it's been theorized that SCP-1121 could be part of a terraforming experiment. If that is the case, one is left to wonder just what it was supposed to turn us into. If it's even us they were after.
- Dr. ███████.

To this:

After 1121-T-5, it's been theorized that SCP-1121 could be part of a terraforming experiment. If that is the case, one is left to wonder just what it was supposed to turn us into. If it's even us they were after.
- Dr. ███████.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I desperately wanted to upvote this, because it is very well written; but couldn't. I am not seeing anything particularly anomalous here. The described behaviour seems to be well within the documented effects of real life pathogens; and body-horror squick isn't enough. Anything that appears to actually be anomalous has been [REDACTED] out of the article. The "terraforming" comment at the end is a complete non sequitor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Euuuurrrghghghghhhhhh.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Staff Notice: this user has been permanently banned for trolling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the compliments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So I realize I'm amazingly late on this, but someone linked it in chat and I feel like commenting. I like everything about this except for the "it's from space" angle, even with the terraforming theory. Reason being that chalking it up to something so…mundane (relatively speaking) somehow takes away from the horror factor of people skinning themselves. It's too much like, say, The Andromeda Strain, when I get the feeling what you wanted was more 28 Days Later. But still, well-executed, and basically anything that makes people rip their skins off is going to get a +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So glad that I found this via random SCP function, as I'd never seen it before. It's damn creepy, ain't it?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I always upvote anything that makes me think "okay, that was really well-done and all, but now I am running the hell away." +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Manis javanica

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A thunder shared is a thunder…ehh…halved?

Anyway, that's pretty much what I got out of your advice too, Smapti.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If Crayne doesn't mind my stealing a bit of his thunder, the "terraforming" bit was my idea.

The "origin story" I had in mind was that, billions of years ago, some dying species on some far-off planet decided that their only hope of saving anything of themselves was to spread their genome to another planet. So they engineered this organism that would, in the presence of bacteria similar enough to those on their own world, be able to mutate living organisms and replace the native DNA with their own. Having developed it, they launched it off into space in as many directions as they could in the hopes that, someday, it might hit a planet where it would stick.

Of course, how much of that is canon in terms of this SCP is up to Crayne, and to the reader, but that's what I had in mind when I was giving him advice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Took out the list of conversational topics anyway. Figured you were right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Both?

The inspiration for this SCP was a visual of some guy sitting in the dark, television blaring out inane infomercials in the background (perhaps one involving falling cups and a dwarf). He pops a Reese's Peanutbutter Cup in his mouth and with the same crusted hand picks at a piece of skin on his scalp, slowly peeling it off.

I can't for the life of me imagine anyone wanting to skin themselves, but I can see the morbid attraction in watching it. Which doesn't really make me sound like the most mentally stable person in the world either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the praise. Having the amoeba be the SCP was definitely a conscious decision. I felt it should be a sort of catalyst for something bigger, with a very specific role to fulfill. What exactly it was supposed to do and what it once targeted I'm not sure of either, but I can imagine a whole range of icky scenarios.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wonderfully horrifying. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Regular or extinct?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure if I am more bothered by the statement itself or that I understand where you are coming from D:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No lie, if this wasn't stated to be extremely painful, I would totally be ok with this happening to me. It sounds wonderful .

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't actually get the terraforming note, but that said, those last two notes really hit this whole thing home. It's horrifying and disturbing in all the right ways, pure classic SCP. I particularly love that it's not the disease, but the amoeba that's the SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This actually made me feel sick. Good job! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For some reason, this reminded of the scene in "Family Guy" when Quagmire says "What's all this skin doing on my arm?" and the proceeds to remove it.

Humorous references aside, this made… well, my skin crawl.

+1 for you, sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"I'm not sure I can't stand this."

That sentence is just wrong. Unless my mind doesn't work.

No, your mind works fine. Mine didn't when I wrote that. Thanks for spotting it. And for the upvote of course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"I'm not sure I can't stand this."

That sentence is just wrong. Unless my mind doesn't work.

+1 anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. Sock puppet accounts work of course. *cough*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted that comment. Mentally in any case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh dear God, the last two lines of that first interview — ! +1 for making me want to curl up into a little ball. Another +1 for the terraforming implications, if I could vote twice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does this mean you found the article… a-peeling?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: With a pangolin no less.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Combine these two, maybe don't refer to the SCP as it, makes it slightly awkward in this case, at least for me.

Fixed. You're right on the use of 'it'.

This seems unnecessary.

I know, I explained somewhere else on the page why I'm choosing to keep it. I understand what you're saying though.

akin is used a lot in SCP articles, I don't think it's tonal, not up to me, carry on.

I'm inclined to agree with you there, never really thought about it. Actually the whole remark about it resembling suffering third-degree burns was a bit off, so I simply took it all out. Didn't add anything.

This doesn't need to be documented.

Perhaps you're right. I was thinking it gave the poor D-Class personnel a bit more humanity, but I suppose it could be replaced with a more clinical observation. I'll think about it…

Addendum 1121-4: Not feeling anything here. The article clarified earlier that this is bio death from space, and this doesn't add to that.
Addendum 1121-3: What you were trying to accomplish with this? I think I see it but it's just way too much of a stretch for me to get behind.

I'm kind of fond of those, so I'm going to leave them in. Which isn't to say I don't understand what put you off about them.

In any case, thanks for replying in such detail. This is the kind of criticism that forces me to think about the choices I've made in an article…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, that was indeed a compliment, as was the +1 I gave it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ehhh…thanks I guess? ;) And yes, Peeled is awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is horrible, and playing the old "Replace [REDACTED] with something humorous" only kept making it worse and worse.
Of course, i mean "horrible" and "worse" in the sense that it made me squirm in all the ways a creepy/horrifying SCP is supposed to.
I'm also thoroughly reminded of Peeled.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice, Crayne. Even better than the draft I read. Love the terraforming bit. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This made me feel uncomfortable, in a good way. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He removed his skin with a [REDACTED]?! OH NO

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gripes
– hide block

with very specific mutagenic qualities. It only reacts

Combine these two, maybe don't refer to the SCP as it, makes it slightly awkward in this case, at least for me.

SCP-1121-1 has proven resistant to all known antibiotics and at this time no known cure for infections with Staphylococcus epidermidis exitiabilis exists, although theoretically the application of SCP-500 should have the expected result. As of yet, authorization of tests to confirm or deny this has not been granted.

This seems unnecessary.

akin to suffering third-degree burns.

akin is used a lot in SCP articles, I don't think it's tonal, not up to me, carry on.

Topics include the test, their various reasons for ending up as D-Class, their spouses and sports. Conversation becomes increasingly erratic as more skin is removed. No hostility towards other test subjects noted.

This doesn't need to be documented.

Addendum 1121-4: Not feeling anything here. The article clarified earlier that this is bio death from space, and this doesn't add to that.
Addendum 1121-3: What you were trying to accomplish with this? I think I see it but it's just way too much of a stretch for me to get behind.

I like the idea of a biological hazard from space, but the concept doesn't flow too well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aha, fair enough. So even in the original outbreak, the specimens were contained before progressing past stage 3? That makes a bit more sense then…
Also, I see your point about non-airborne pathogens, but most pathogens don't make people actively infect anyone who isn't already. To put it another way, imagine if Ebola wasn't airborne, but it actively caused its carriers to walk into the most crowded areas they can find and stand around until they crash and bleed out. So my vote's still for Keter. Even if it doesn't go airborne, it's still incredibly virulent. One little nick in a researcher's glove, and there's at LEAST one major population center gone. And like I said, it's a very potent threat to people's sense of normalcy.

Oh, one more thing… kudos for making this thing work by mutating one of the most common benign pathogens in the human body. That stuff's literally on almost everyone's skin, just chilling out.

……. I just realized something. The peeling off of their skin and seeing nothing wrong with it? They're molting. MAJOR Fridge Horror moment for me there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
a: Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus is abbreviated SCBA. SBCA is the Satellite Broadcasting and Communications Association.

Noted, will fix.

b: lose the reference to SCP-500. it's obvious, it doesn't add anything, and cross-scp references are bad to start with.

If I lose the reference, there's the risk of someone popping in to say: "dude, SCP-500 cures everything!" Also, I put it in specifically to show that this apparently isn't worthy enough to test SCP-500 on. As in: the Foundation really doesn't care about possibly curing infected civilians. Firebomb the fuck out of those wretches and be done with it.

c: there are two different bugs here - the protozoan, and the mutant staph. the article would be easier to read if they were 1121-1 and 1121-2, rather than 1121 and 1121-1.

I understand where you're coming from, but I want it to remain clear that what we refer to as SCP-XXXX is the parent of XXXX-1.

d: the protozoan doesn't reproduce in any way, but if it finds staph (somehow), it creates a transmissible, self sustaining infection? this ecology doesn't make much sense.

It's not supposed to. It's not a natural thing, someone doctored this up in order to life on earth into something…else.

e: the blackouts are fine, but none of the redactions and expungements seem necessary. they aren't adding anything.

Since you're the second person to come up with that, I'll seriously consider either taking them out or reworking their use. I guess I kind of feel it's not a good SCP if I don't expunge SOMETHING. Fucked up, I know.

f: the actual effects are a bit lacking. you rip off your skin and turn into a zombie. and then…? you've got some vague hints about hive activity (and maybe a reason). expand on that.

You rip off your skin, you partially mutate and yes, there's hints about the absence of a hive mind. I'd rather leave it hanging there, with the rest being up to the reader's imagination.

g: the tone and quality drop off after the first addendum.

Can you point me to specific parts where you'd reword?

h: firebombing the discovery site seems really clumsy, and overspecific.

Noted. I wanted to show that they didn't want any personnel in the area after the outbreak and simply sent in airstrikes, but I can see where it might be a bit too specific. I'll leave it in for now. At least until EchoFourDelta has declared it to be complete and utter bullshit. There always the hope he doesn't though. *grins*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i like this - it reads pretty well - but i have a number of comments:

a: Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus is abbreviated SCBA. SBCA is the Satellite Broadcasting and Communications Association.

b: lose the reference to SCP-500. it's obvious, it doesn't add anything, and cross-scp references are bad to start with.

c: there are two different bugs here - the protozoan, and the mutant staph. the article would be easier to read if they were 1121-1 and 1121-2, rather than 1121 and 1121-1.

d: the protozoan doesn't reproduce in any way, but if it finds staph (somehow), it creates a transmissible, self sustaining infection? this ecology doesn't make much sense.

e: the blackouts are fine, but none of the redactions and expungements seem necessary. they aren't adding anything.

f: the actual effects are a bit lacking. you rip off your skin and turn into a zombie. and then…? you've got some vague hints about hive activity (and maybe a reason). expand on that.

g: the tone and quality drop off after the first addendum.

h: firebombing the discovery site seems really clumsy, and overspecific.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off, thanks.

The reason I didn't go with Keter is that (and Smapti clued me into this) non-airborne pathogens are relatively easily contained (and I use the word 'lightly' quite lightly here). However, since it's stated that infection has spread without skin-to-skin contact being observed too, it MIGHT qualify for Keter.

I'm on the fence on that.

As for what happens after stage 3…I think keeping that in the dark is the point. If we allow a specimen to fully form, what then? Does it telepathically sound a call for something across the great dark void? Does it explode like those tankbugs in Starship Troopers? I'm thinking the Foundation doesn't really care to find out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, first of all, JESUS. This is absolute delightful awesomeness. Plus, you gave your horrible squicky microorganism earwig DNA. Second, I'd… probably vote Keter. If it gets out of containment we're looking at something potentially even more infectious than Ebola Zaire, and we'd be administering Class A amnesiacs for YEARS trying to restore the public's belief in normalcy. Yes, it's currently very well contained, but so are a lot of nasty horrible diseases that would wreck everyone's day if there was even a minute breach at a hot lab.

I would like to see a -little- more expansion though. What happens after stage 3? They're just… transformed into these skinless wriggling dudes and walk around like nothing happened, barring their desire to attack and thereby infect anyone who isn't a skinless wriggling monstrosity? Or does something further happen after a while into stage 3?

By the way, that first test log interview is absolutely fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. To Maytne too. And to think I actually trimmed it down a bit too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This, I like this, a lot.

Amazing work, not too scary or squicky but most certainly interesting.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I.. Oh god. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks man, it was the input you and bbaztek gave me that set this one on the right track. :)

That said, I'm unsure if this should be Euclid or Keter. Any thoughts?
